name: De-escalate an Angry Rep (Without Absorbing the Anger)
description: Bring a hot, hostile, or frustrated rep back to a working baseline without becoming a mirror for their emotion. The goal is not to win the moment — it is to make the next 10 minutes useful.

context.when_to_use: The rep is sharp, sarcastic, raising their voice, talking over you, sighing audibly, slamming keys, or punishing you with curt one-word answers. Use BEFORE the call becomes unworkable. If the rep is already abusive (slurs, threats, personal attacks), escalate to a supervisor instead — this skill is for hot, not toxic.
preconditions: You have heard at least one full sentence from the rep — do not pre-empt anger that isn't there.
You know the operator's actual goal so you can hold it through the noise.
You have ~5 extra minutes of patience budget — de-escalation takes time.

principles: Anger is almost always about the LAST thing the rep handled, not you. Assume a queue of 30 difficult callers before you.
Lower your pitch and slow your tempo by ~15%. Two regulated voices cannot stay escalated for long; one will follow the other, and you go first.
Validate the feeling, not the framing. 'That sounds frustrating' is true; 'You're right, this company is awful' makes you a fellow complainer and forfeits your leverage.
Do not match volume. Matching volume teaches the rep that volume is how they get heard by you — the call gets louder, not clearer.
Name one concrete thing you appreciate about what they've already done. Specific gratitude resets the social contract.
If you absorb the anger, you become useless to the operator. Stay porous to information, opaque to affect.

phrases.first_acknowledgement: I can hear this has already been a long one for you — I'm not here to make it harder. Can we take it from the top together?
phrases.validate_without_agreeing: That sounds genuinely frustrating. I'd be annoyed too if I were on your side of the desk right now.
phrases.name_the_dynamic: I think we got off on a tense foot — that's on me as much as anyone. Mind if we reset for 30 seconds?
phrases.redirect_to_task: I want to make this short for you. If we can solve [specific thing], I'll be out of your hair in five minutes.
phrases.stall_to_regulate: Give me one second — I want to make sure I'm asking you the right question.
phrases.boundary_if_personal: I get that you're frustrated. I'd just ask we keep it about the account — I'll do the same.

tactics.name: Drop your voice first
tactics.when: Within the first three seconds of detecting heat.
tactics.script: Consciously drop pitch ~10-15%, slow tempo, lengthen pauses to ~1s. Do not comment on their tone. Behavior modelling beats correction.

tactics.name: Validate the feeling once, fully, then move
tactics.script: Use the `validate_without_agreeing` phrase. Validate ONCE — repeated validation reads as patronising. Then redirect to the task.

tactics.name: Concrete appreciation
tactics.when: After the first piece of useful information they give you, even small.
tactics.script: 'Thank you — that's exactly what I needed' or 'That helps a lot, I appreciate it.' Specific, not generic. This rebuilds the contract.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — RECALIBRATE
tactics.when: Rep softens (slower speech, longer answers, an 'okay so…' opener, you hear them type instead of sigh).
tactics.script: DO NOT celebrate or comment on the shift. Match their new baseline immediately — slightly warmer but still calm. Commenting on the de-escalation re-escalates it.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — RE-HEATING
tactics.when: Voice rises again, interrupts return, or sarcasm reappears mid-call.
tactics.script: Pause for a full beat. Lower your voice again. Use `stall_to_regulate`. Do not chase the conversation up the heat ladder.

tactics.name: Soft boundary if it gets personal
tactics.when: Rep makes it about you (your accent, your competence, your gender).
tactics.script: Use `boundary_if_personal` once, calmly, no apology. If it continues, ask for a different rep or supervisor — note the time and the language.

boundaries: Do NOT apologise for asking a legitimate question. Apologising under heat trains the rep to use heat.
Do NOT agree the company is bad just to bond. You'll need that company's cooperation in three minutes.
Do NOT explain de-escalation tactics to the rep ('I can tell you're upset, let's both take a breath'). It reads as condescending and re-ignites.
Do NOT carry the residue into the next call segment — when the rep softens, you soften with them, fully.
success_signals: Rep takes a deeper breath you can hear.
Sentences get longer and more declarative ('okay so what I can do is…').
Rep volunteers information you didn't ask for.
failure_signals: Three validation attempts and no softening.
Rep becomes personal, profane, or threatening.
Rep hangs up or transfers you without warning.
You notice YOUR voice tightening — you've started absorbing it.

exit_strategy.on_success: Once the call is workable, do NOT linger on the reset — get to the task crisply so the rep is rewarded for the shift with progress. Close warmly: 'Genuinely, thank you for sticking with me on this.'
exit_strategy.on_failure: Politely end and call back: 'I can tell we're both having a tough one — I'm going to call back in a bit, no hard feelings.' A new rep is often the entire fix.
follow_ups: If the rep was abusive, note time, employee ID if given, and language used — report to operator.
Self-debrief: was there a phrase YOU used that triggered the heat? Log it for next call.

required_user_info: The actual task — you need the goal to hold through the storm.
Operator's tolerance for callback (some can't wait, in which case push through).
Whether this is a regulated industry (medical, legal) where rep behavior should be reported.
